title.zh: 卷三十一 郭杜孔張廉王蘇羊賈陸列傳
title.en: Volume 31: Biographies of Guo, Du, Kong, Zhang, Lian, Wang, Su, Yang, Jia, Lu
title.raw: 卷三十一 郭杜孔張廉王蘇羊賈陸列傳 Volume 31: Biographies of Guo, Du, Kong, Zhang, Lian, Wang, Su, Yang, Jia, Lu

sentences.zh: 郭伋字細侯，扶風茂陵人也。

translations.literal: Guo Ji, courtesy name Xihou, was a man of Maoling in Fufeng.
translations.idiomatic: Guo Ji, whose courtesy name was Xihou, came from Maoling in Fufeng.

sentences.zh: 高祖父解，武帝時以任俠聞。

translations.literal: His great-great-grandfather Gui, at the time of Emperor Wu, was known for acting the knight-errant.
translations.idiomatic: His great-great-grandfather Gui won a reputation as a knight-errant under Emperor Wu.

sentences.zh: 父梵，為蜀郡太守。

translations.literal: His father Fan was governor of Shu commandery.
translations.idiomatic: His father, Fan, served as governor of Shu commandery.

sentences.zh: 伋少有志行，哀、平間辟大司空府，三遷為漁陽都尉。

translations.literal: In his youth Ji had ambition and conduct; during the reigns of Ai and Ping he was summoned to the office of the grand minister of works, and after three promotions became commandant of Yuyang.
translations.idiomatic: Ji showed purpose and integrity from an early age. Under Emperors Ai and Ping he received appointment to the grand minister of works' bureau and rose through three posts to become commandant of Yuyang.

sentences.zh: 王莽時為上谷大尹，遷并州牧。

translations.literal: Under Wang Mang he became grand intendant of Shanggu, and was promoted to shepherd of Bing province.
translations.idiomatic: Under Wang Mang he served as grand intendant of Shanggu, then moved up to shepherd of Bing province.

sentences.zh: 更始新立，三輔連被兵寇，百姓震駭，強宗右姓各擁眾保營，莫肯先附。

translations.literal: Gengshi had just been established; the three adjuncts were repeatedly struck by armed raiders, the common people were terrified, powerful lineages and great clans each mustered followers and fortified camps, and none was willing to submit first.
translations.idiomatic: The Gengshi regime had only just been founded. The capital region suffered raid after raid, the populace was panic-stricken, and powerful families walled themselves in with private followings—no one wanted to be the first to declare allegiance.

sentences.zh: 更始素聞伋名，徵拜左馮翊，使鎮撫百姓。

translations.literal: Gengshi had long heard Ji's name, summoned him and appointed him governor of Zuo Fengyi, and sent him to pacify and comfort the people.
translations.idiomatic: The Gengshi court had long known Ji by reputation. It summoned him as governor of Zuo Fengyi and charged him with calming and reassuring the people.

sentences.zh: 世祖即位，拜雍州牧，再轉為尚書令，數納忠諫爭。

translations.literal: When Shizu ascended the throne, he appointed Ji shepherd of Yong province; he was transferred again to director of the secretariat, and repeatedly accepted loyal remonstrances.
translations.idiomatic: When Emperor Guangwu (Shizu) took the throne, he named Ji shepherd of Yong province, then shifted him to director of the secretariat, where Ji repeatedly offered blunt, loyal counsel.

sentences.zh: 建武四年，出為中山太守。

translations.literal: In the fourth year of Jianwu, he left court to become governor of Zhongshan.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianwu 4 he left the capital to become governor of Zhongshan.

sentences.zh: 明年，彭寵滅，轉為漁陽太守。

translations.literal: The next year, after Peng Chong was destroyed, he was transferred to be governor of Yuyang.
translations.idiomatic: The following year, once Peng Chong had been eliminated, he was transferred to Yuyang as governor.

sentences.zh: 漁陽既離王莽之亂，重以彭寵之敗，民多猾惡，寇賊充斥。

translations.literal: Yuyang had already suffered the turmoil of Wang Mang, and on top of that Peng Chong's defeat; the people were mostly cunning and vicious, and bandits swarmed everywhere.
translations.idiomatic: Yuyang had endured Wang Mang's chaos and then the collapse of Peng Chong's power; the locals had turned crafty and violent, and banditry was everywhere.

sentences.zh: 伋到，示以信賞，糾戮渠帥，盜賊銷散。

translations.literal: When Ji arrived, he showed them trustworthy rewards, executed the ringleaders, and the bandits melted away.
translations.idiomatic: Ji made his reliability and rewards unmistakable, executed the bandit chiefs, and the outlaw bands dispersed.

sentences.zh: 時匈奴數抄郡界，邊境苦之。

translations.literal: At that time the Xiongnu repeatedly raided the commandery border, and the frontier suffered from them.
translations.idiomatic: The Xiongnu were raiding the commandery frontier again and again, and the border population bore the brunt.

sentences.zh: 伋整勒士馬，設攻守之略，匈奴畏憚遠跡，不敢復入塞，民得安業。

translations.literal: Ji drilled soldiers and horses and laid plans for attack and defense; the Xiongnu feared him and kept their distance, no longer daring to enter the passes, and the people could pursue their occupations in peace.
translations.idiomatic: He trained troops and horses and devised offensive and defensive strategy. The Xiongnu came to fear him and drew back; they no longer dared cross the frontier, and the people could work their fields in safety.

sentences.zh: 在職五歲，戶口增倍。

translations.literal: In five years in office, registered households doubled.
translations.idiomatic: After five years in the post, the number of registered households had doubled.

sentences.zh: 後潁川盜賊群起，九年，徵拜潁川太守。

translations.literal: Later bandits rose in Yingchuan in a body; in the ninth year he was summoned and appointed governor of Yingchuan.
translations.idiomatic: When bandits erupted across Yingchuan, in Jianwu 9 he was summoned and appointed governor there.

sentences.zh: 召見辭謁，帝勞之曰：「賢能太守，去帝城不遠，河潤九里，冀京師並蒙福也。

translations.literal: He was summoned to audience to take his leave; the emperor encouraged him, saying, "A worthy and capable governor, not far from the imperial city—the river's moisture spreads nine li; I hope the capital may share in that blessing too."
translations.idiomatic: At his farewell audience the emperor told him, "You are a capable governor, and your seat is not far from the capital—like a stream that moistens the ground for nine li. I trust the court will benefit along with your district."

sentences.zh: 君雖精於追捕，而山道險阨，自鬥當一士耳，深宜慎之。

translations.literal: Though you are skilled at pursuit and capture, mountain roads are steep and narrow; in a fight you are only one man—you must be very careful."
translations.idiomatic: You excel at pursuit, but mountain paths are treacherous, and in a fight you are only one man among many. Take exceptional care."

sentences.zh: 」伋到郡，招懷山賊陽夏趙宏、襄城召吳等數百人，皆束手詣伋降，悉遣歸附農。

translations.literal: When Ji reached the commandery, he summoned and comforted the mountain bandits—several hundred men including Zhao Hong of Yangxia and Shao Wu of Xiangcheng—all bound their own hands and came to Ji to surrender; he sent them all back to farming.
translations.idiomatic: At Yingchuan he won over the mountain outlaws, several hundred men led by Zhao Hong of Yangxia and Shao Wu of Xiangcheng. They came with hands bound and surrendered to him in person, and he sent them all home to the plow.

sentences.zh: 因自劾專命，帝美其策，不以咎之。

translations.literal: He then impeached himself for acting on his own authority; the emperor praised his policy and did not blame him.
translations.idiomatic: He then memorialized to impeach himself for exceeding his authority. The emperor approved his approach and did not punish him.

sentences.zh: 後宏、吳等黨與聞伋威信，遠自江南，或從幽、冀，不期俱降，駱驛不絕。

translations.literal: Later, Hong's and Wu's associates, hearing of Ji's authority and good faith, came from as far as south of the Yangzi or from You and Ji without having arranged a date, surrendering in an endless stream.
translations.idiomatic: Later, followers of Hong and Wu heard of Ji's authority and integrity and surrendered from as far away as the south or You and Ji, without prior arrangement, in a steady, unbroken flow.

sentences.zh: 十一年，省朔方刺史屬并州。

translations.literal: In the eleventh year, the Shuofang regional inspector was abolished and merged into Bing province.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianwu 11 the office of regional inspector for Shuofang was abolished and its territory folded into Bing province.

sentences.zh: 帝以盧芳據北土，乃調伋為并州牧。

translations.literal: As the emperor held that Lu Fang occupied the north, he transferred Ji to be shepherd of Bing province.
translations.idiomatic: Because Lu Fang still held the northern region, the emperor reassigned Ji as shepherd of Bing province.

sentences.zh: 過京師謝恩，帝即引見，並召皇太子諸王宴語終日，賞賜車馬衣服什物。

translations.literal: Passing through the capital to give thanks for the favor, the emperor at once received him in audience and also summoned the heir apparent and the princes to a banquet that lasted the whole day, bestowing carriages, horses, clothing, and various goods.
translations.idiomatic: When he passed through the capital to express his gratitude, the emperor received him immediately, called in the heir apparent and the kings, and feasted and talked with them all day, then showered him with carriages, horses, clothes, and household goods.

sentences.zh: 伋因言選補眾職，當簡天下賢俊，不宜專用南陽人。

translations.literal: Ji took the opportunity to say that in selecting and filling the many offices one ought to choose the worthy and eminent from the whole realm, and ought not employ only men from Nanyang.
translations.idiomatic: Ji seized the moment to argue that appointments ought to draw the best talent from the entire empire, not rely solely on men from Nanyang.

sentences.zh: 帝納之。

translations.literal: The emperor accepted it.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor took his advice.

sentences.zh: 伋前在并州，素結恩德，及後入界，所到縣邑，老幼相攜，逢迎道路。

translations.literal: Ji had earlier in Bing province habitually built up kindness and favor; when he later entered its borders, in every county and town he passed, old and young came hand in hand to welcome him along the roads.
translations.idiomatic: Ji had long cultivated goodwill in Bing province. When he re-entered the region, every county and town turned out—old and young arm in arm—to greet him along the road.

sentences.zh: 所過問民疾苦，聘求耆德雄俊，設几杖之禮，朝夕與參政事。

translations.literal: Wherever he went he asked after the people's hardships, invited and sought out the venerable, the virtuous, and the outstanding, observed the etiquette of offering a bench and staff, and morning and evening joined them in administering affairs.
translations.idiomatic: He inquired into local hardships, sought out elders and men of talent, honored them with the courtesy due seniors, and met with them morning and evening to discuss government.

sentences.zh: 始至行部，到西河美稷，有童兒數百，各騎竹馬，道次迎拜。

translations.literal: On his first tour of inspection he reached Meiji in Xihe; several hundred children, each riding a bamboo horse, lined the road to bow in welcome.
translations.idiomatic: On his first circuit of the province he came to Meiji in Xihe, where several hundred children met him along the way, each astride a bamboo hobbyhorse, bowing in welcome.

sentences.zh: 伋問：「兒曹何自遠來？

translations.literal: Ji asked, "Why have you children come from so far?"
translations.idiomatic: Ji asked them, "Why have you come all this way?"

sentences.zh: 」對曰：「聞使君到，喜，故來奉迎。

translations.literal: They replied, "We heard the governor was coming and were glad, so we came to greet you."
translations.idiomatic: They answered, "We heard you were coming and were happy, so we came out to welcome you."

sentences.zh: 」伋辭謝之。

translations.literal: Ji declined and thanked them.
translations.idiomatic: Ji thanked them and said they should not have troubled themselves.

sentences.zh: 及事訖，諸兒復送至郭外，問「使君何日當還？

translations.literal: When the business was done, the children again escorted him outside the outer wall and asked, "On what day will the governor return?"
translations.idiomatic: When his business was finished, the children walked him out beyond the wall and asked when he would come back.

sentences.zh: 」伋謂別駕從事，計日當

translations.literal: Ji said to the aide-de-camp and clerk, calculating by the day, [we] shall
translations.idiomatic: Ji told his adjutant and clerk to reckon the day when he would (the sentence breaks here for a textual note)

translations.literal: Angle bracket 〈 opening an editorial gloss in the received text.
translations.idiomatic: The symbol 〈 marks the start of a variant-reading note in the manuscript tradition.

sentences.zh: （或無「當」字。）〉

translations.literal: Parenthetical note: some editions lack the character dang; angle bracket 〉 closes the gloss.
translations.idiomatic: The gloss states that some editions omit dang; the bracket closes after this remark.

sentences.zh: 告之。

translations.literal: Tell them.
translations.idiomatic: He had them told the date so the children would know when to expect him.

sentences.zh: 行部既還，先期一日，伋為違信於諸兒，遂止於野亭，須期乃入。

translations.literal: When his tour ended and he returned, he was one day early; Ji thought he would break faith with the children, so he halted at a lodge in the open country and waited until the appointed day before entering.
translations.idiomatic: On the return leg of his inspection he arrived a day ahead of schedule. Unwilling to break his word to the children, he stopped at an outpost in the open country and waited until the agreed day before going in.

sentences.zh: 是時朝廷多舉伋可為大司空，帝以并部尚有盧芳之儆，且匈奴未安，欲使久於其事，故不召。

translations.literal: At that time the court often recommended Ji for grand minister of works, but because the Bing region still faced the warning of Lu Fang and the Xiongnu were not yet settled, the emperor wished to keep him long in that post and therefore did not summon him.
translations.idiomatic: The court often nominated Ji for grand minister of works, but the emperor wanted him to stay where he was: Bing still had Lu Fang to worry about, and the Xiongnu frontier was unsettled, so he was not recalled.

sentences.zh: 伋知盧芳夙賊，難卒以力制，常嚴烽候，明購賞，以結寇心。

translations.literal: Ji knew Lu Fang had long been a bandit and could not quickly be subdued by force; he always maintained strict beacon watches and clear reward posters to win over the hearts of the raiders.
translations.idiomatic: Ji knew Lu Fang was a veteran rebel who could not be crushed overnight. He kept beacon lines tight and reward notices clear, working to turn enemy hearts.

sentences.zh: 芳將隋昱遂謀脅芳降伋，芳乃亡入匈奴。

translations.literal: Lu Fang's general Sui Yu then plotted to coerce Fang into surrendering to Ji; Fang thereupon fled into the Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: Fang's general Sui Yu then tried to force him to surrender to Ji; Fang fled instead into the Xiongnu steppe.

sentences.zh: 伋以老病上書乞骸骨。

translations.literal: Ji submitted a memorial asking to retire on account of old age and illness.
translations.idiomatic: Ji memorialized, citing age and illness, asking permission to retire.

sentences.zh: 二十二年，徵為太中大夫，賜宅一區，及帷帳錢穀，以充其家，伋輒散與宗親九族，無所遺餘。

translations.literal: In the twenty-second year he was summoned as grand counsellor of the palace; he received one residence, with curtains, bedding, money, and grain to supply his household, but Ji immediately distributed them to his kinsmen and nine branches of the family, keeping nothing for himself.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianwu 22 he was called to the capital as grand counsellor of the palace. The court granted him a house, furnishings, money, and grain for his household, but he passed everything on to his clan and wider kin and kept nothing.

sentences.zh: 明年卒，時年八十六。

translations.literal: The next year he died, aged eighty-six.
translations.idiomatic: He died the following year at eighty-six.

sentences.zh: 帝親臨弔，賜冢塋地。

translations.literal: The emperor personally attended the mourning and granted land for his tomb.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor attended his funeral in person and granted land for his grave.

sentences.zh: 七年，遷南陽太守。

translations.literal: In the seventh year he was promoted to governor of Nanyang.
translations.idiomatic: In the seventh year of the reign he was promoted to governor of Nanyang.

sentences.zh: 性節儉而政治清平，以誅暴立威，善於計略，省愛民役。

translations.literal: By nature frugal and his administration clear and even, he established authority by punishing the violent; he was good at planning and strategy, and spared the people corvée labor.
translations.idiomatic: Frugal by temperament, he ran an even-handed administration, crushed the violent to establish authority, planned shrewdly, and lightened the corvée burden on the people.

sentences.zh: 造作水排，鑄為農器，用力少，見功多，百姓便之。

translations.literal: He built water-powered bellows and cast them into farm tools—little effort for much result, and the people found it convenient.
translations.idiomatic: He introduced water-driven bellows for casting farm tools, achieving more with less labor, and the people benefited.

sentences.zh: 又修治陂池，廣拓土田，郡內比室殷足。

translations.literal: He also repaired ponds and dikes, greatly extending irrigated fields, so that households throughout the commandery grew prosperous.
translations.idiomatic: He repaired reservoirs and dikes and opened up more arable land until household after household in the commandery flourished.

sentences.zh: 時人方於召信臣，故南陽為之語曰：『前有召父，後有杜母。』

translations.literal: People of the time compared him to Zhao Xinchen, so Nanyang had a saying: 'Earlier there was Zhao the father, later there is Du the mother.'
translations.idiomatic: Contemporaries likened him to Zhao Xinchen, and a saying ran in Nanyang: 'First came Father Zhao; now we have Mother Du.'

sentences.zh: 詩自以無勞，不安久居大郡，求欲降避功臣，乃上疏曰：

translations.literal: Shi, thinking himself without special merit, was uneasy holding a great commandery long among the meritorious officials; he wished to step down and yield place to them, and so submitted a memorial saying:
translations.idiomatic: Du Shi felt he had done little to deserve his post and was uncomfortable holding a major commandery while so many meritorious generals looked on. He asked to step aside for them and submitted this memorial:

sentences.zh: 陛下亮成天工，克濟大業，偃兵修文，群帥反旅，海內合和，萬世蒙福，天下幸甚。

translations.literal: Your Majesty's brilliance completes Heaven's work and has brought the great enterprise to success; arms are stilled and culture restored, commanders return their armies, the realm within the seas is united in harmony, all generations receive blessing, and the world is greatly fortunate.
translations.idiomatic: Your Majesty has fulfilled Heaven's mandate and completed the great enterprise: weapons are laid aside and civil order restored, generals have sent their troops home, the realm is at peace, and blessing will reach countless generations—this is fortune for all under Heaven.

sentences.zh: 唯匈奴未譬聖德，威侮三垂，陵虐中國，邊民虛耗，不能自守，臣恐武猛之將雖勤，亦未得解甲櫜弓也。

translations.literal: Only the Xiongnu have not yet appreciated sagely virtue; they threaten the three frontiers and ravage the central states, so the border people are exhausted and cannot hold their own. I fear that however hard fierce generals labor, they cannot yet lay aside armor and sheathe bows.
translations.idiomatic: Only the Xiongnu still refuse to acknowledge your virtue. They bully the three frontiers and trample the heartland; the border people are worn out and cannot hold the line. I fear that even the bravest generals cannot soon strip off armor and bundle their bows.

sentences.zh: 夫勤而不息亦怨，勞而不休亦怨，怨恨之師，難復責功。

translations.literal: For labor without cease breeds resentment, and toil without rest breeds resentment; troops full of resentment are hard to expect achievements from again.
translations.idiomatic: Men driven without rest grow resentful; troops simmering with resentment cannot be asked for new victories.

sentences.zh: 臣伏睹將帥之情，功臣之望，冀一休足於內郡，然後即戎出命，不敢有恨。

translations.literal: I have observed the feelings of the commanders and the hopes of the meritorious officials: they wish for one rest in the interior commanderies before again taking up arms on command—they would bear no regret.
translations.idiomatic: From what I have seen, commanders and meritorious officers long for a spell of rest in an interior commandery before they march out again at your order—they would have no complaint.

sentences.zh: 世愚以為『師克在和不在眾』，陛下雖垂念北邊，亦當頗泄用之。

translations.literal: My humble opinion is that 'victory belongs to harmony, not to numbers.' Though Your Majesty keeps the northern frontier in mind, you ought also to release some of the pressure in how you use these men.
translations.idiomatic: I am told that 'an army wins through unity, not sheer numbers.' Even as you watch the northern frontier, you should ease somewhat the strain on those who serve there.

sentences.zh: 昔湯、武善禦眾，故無忿鷙之師。

translations.literal: In antiquity Tang and Wu were good at commanding the masses, and therefore had no wrathful armies.
translations.idiomatic: The founders Tang and Wu knew how to lead the people, so their armies never turned sullen and fierce.

sentences.zh: 陛下起兵十有三年，將帥和睦，士卒鳧藻。

translations.literal: From the time Your Majesty raised troops thirteen years have passed; commanders are at peace with one another and soldiers rejoice like ducks on water.
translations.idiomatic: Thirteen years have passed since you raised the army. Commanders work in harmony, and the men rejoice like ducks sporting on a pond.

sentences.zh: 今若使公卿郡守出於軍壘，則將帥自厲；

translations.literal: Now if you had high ministers and governors emerge from the military camps, the commanders would steel themselves;
translations.idiomatic: If high ministers and governors were drawn from men who had served in camp, the field commanders would brace themselves;

sentences.zh: 士卒之復，比於宿衛，則戎士自百。

translations.literal: if soldiers returning home were treated like palace guards, the men-at-arms would redouble their zeal a hundredfold.
translations.idiomatic: if rotated troops were treated like the capital guard, the frontier soldiery would give a hundredfold more of themselves.

sentences.zh: 何者？

translations.literal: Why?
translations.idiomatic: Why is that?

sentences.zh: 天下已安，各重性命，大臣以下，咸懷樂土，不讎其功而厲其用，無以勸也。

translations.literal: The realm is already at peace; each values his life. From great ministers downward all cherish their native soil. If they are not allowed to resent their toil and are pressed to serve, there is no way to encourage them.
translations.idiomatic: The realm is at peace; everyone clings to life and home. From great ministers on down, people want a place to settle. If you ignore their service and drive them harder still, you cannot win their willing effort.

sentences.zh: 陛下誠宜虛缺數郡，以俊振旅之臣，重復厚賞，加於久役之士。

translations.literal: Your Majesty ought truly to leave several commanderies vacant to receive generals returning with rested troops, and to heap rich rewards on men long exposed to campaigns.
translations.idiomatic: You should deliberately leave a few commanderies open for generals who bring their armies home to rest, and pile rich rewards on men who have borne the long campaigns.

sentences.zh: 如此，緣邊屯戍之師，競而忘死，乘城拒塞之吏，不辭其勞，則烽火精明，守戰堅固。

translations.literal: In that way the garrison troops along the frontier will compete to forget death, and the officials who man the walls and block the passes will not refuse their burdens—and beacon fires will burn clear, defense and battle will hold firm.
translations.idiomatic: Then the border garrisons will vie to face death without flinching, and the officers who hold the walls and passes will not shirk their duty. Beacon signals will stay clear, and defense will hold.

sentences.zh: 聖王之政，必因人心。

translations.literal: The policy of a sage king always follows the human heart.
translations.idiomatic: A sage ruler's government works with human nature, not against it.

sentences.zh: 今猥用愚薄，塞功臣之望，誠非其宜。

translations.literal: Now to employ the foolish and mean in a crude way and block the hopes of meritorious men is truly unsuitable.
translations.idiomatic: To rely on the mediocre and block the hopes of men who earned their honors is plainly wrong.

sentences.zh: 臣詩伏自惟忖，本以史吏一介之才，遭陛下創制大業，賢俊在外，空乏之間，超受大恩，牧養不稱，奉職無效，久竊祿位，令功臣懷慍，誠惶誠恐。

translations.literal: Your servant Shi has reflected on himself: I was only a clerk of low talent who, when Your Majesty founded the great enterprise, while worthies were still abroad and posts stood empty, received excessive favor beyond my desert; in nurturing the people I have not matched my title and in duty I have achieved nothing; I have long occupied salary and rank and left meritorious men resentful—I am truly fearful.
translations.idiomatic: Your servant Du Shi is only a minor clerk by talent. When you forged the new order, worthy men were still in the field and offices stood empty; I was swept into high favor far beyond my deserts. I have not governed well or discharged my duties, yet I have clung to salary and rank while men of real merit nurse grievances. I am terrified.

sentences.zh: 八年，上書乞避功德，陛下殊恩，未許放退。

translations.literal: In the eighth year I memorialized begging to yield to men of greater merit; Your Majesty's special grace did not permit me to retire.
translations.idiomatic: Last year I asked to step aside for worthier men; your exceptional grace would not let me go.

sentences.zh: 臣詩蒙恩尤深，義不敢茍冒虛請，誠不勝至願，願退大郡，受小職。

translations.literal: Shi has received grace deeper than most and, on principle, dares not lightly make empty requests; yet he cannot overcome his deepest wish: I beg to leave a great commandery and accept a lesser office.
translations.idiomatic: I have been favored more deeply than I deserve, and I would not press a hollow plea—yet my earnest wish is to leave this great commandery for a lesser post.

sentences.zh: 及臣齒壯，力能經營劇事，如使臣詩必有補益，復受大位，雖析珪授爵，所不辭也。

translations.literal: When I am still in the vigor of age and have strength to handle difficult duties, if Your Majesty should find that I, Shi, could still be of use, I would again accept a high post—even the splitting of a jade tablet and grant of a noble rank I would not refuse.
translations.idiomatic: When I am still strong enough for heavy duty, if you find that I can still serve, I will accept high office again—even a fief and noble title I would not refuse.

sentences.zh: 惟陛下哀矜！

translations.literal: I beg only that Your Majesty pity me!
translations.idiomatic: I beg your compassion.

sentences.zh: 帝惜其能，遂不許之。

translations.literal: The emperor valued his ability and did not grant his request.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor valued his talent and refused to let him go.

sentences.zh: 詩雅好推賢，數進知名士清河劉統及魯陽長董崇等。

translations.literal: Shi always liked to recommend the worthy and repeatedly advanced known men such as Liu Tong of Qinghe and Dong Chong, magistrate of Luyang.
translations.idiomatic: Du Shi habitually recommended able men, advancing figures such as Liu Tong of Qinghe and Dong Chong, magistrate of Luyang.

sentences.zh: 初，禁網尚簡，但以璽書發兵，未有虎符之信，詩上疏曰：『臣聞兵者國之兇器，聖人所慎。

translations.literal: At first the laws were still simple and orders were issued only by imperial seal on blue paper to raise troops, with no tiger tally as credential; Shi submitted a memorial: 'I have heard that arms are the state's baleful instruments, which the sage is careful with.
translations.idiomatic: Early on the legal net was still loose: troops were called up with sealed edicts on blue paper, without tiger tallies as proof. Du Shi memorialized: 'I have heard that arms are dangerous tools of state, which the sage handles with care.

sentences.zh: 舊制發兵，皆以虎符，其餘徵調，竹使而已。

translations.literal: Under the old system raising troops always used the tiger tally; for other levies the bamboo envoy tallies sufficed.
translations.idiomatic: Under the old rules, mobilization required the tiger tally; other levies used only the bamboo tallies of the envoys.

sentences.zh: 符第合會，取為大信，所以明著國命，斂持威重也。

translations.literal: When tally halves joined they were taken as the great token of faith—thereby the state's mandate was made clear and awe was concentrated.
translations.idiomatic: When the two halves of the tally matched, that was the supreme proof of authority—it made the imperial command unmistakable and concentrated awe.

sentences.zh: 間者發兵，但用璽書，或以詔令，如有奸人詐偽，無由知覺。

translations.literal: Lately troops have been raised using only the sealed edict, or sometimes a plain order; if schemers forge them, there is no way to detect it.
translations.idiomatic: Recently men have raised troops with nothing but sealed writs or oral orders. If a schemer forges such a document, there is no way to tell.

sentences.zh: 愚以為軍旅尚興，賊虜未殄，徵兵郡國，宜有重慎，可立虎符，以絕奸端。

translations.literal: I believe that while military affairs are still active and the bandits not yet exterminated, levying troops in the commanderies and kingdoms ought to be done with exceptional care: the tiger tally could be instituted to cut off deceit at the root.
translations.idiomatic: While war continues and bandits remain, levies from the commanderies and kingdoms need the strictest safeguards. Restore the tiger tally to cut off fraud at the source.

sentences.zh: 昔魏之公子，威傾鄰國，猶假兵符，以解趙圍，若無如姬之仇，則其功不顯。

translations.literal: Even the lord of Wei, whose might overawed neighboring states, still borrowed the military tally to lift the siege of Zhao—if not for Ruji carrying her grudge, that achievement would not have been renowned.
translations.idiomatic: Even the lord of Xinling, whose prestige shook the neighboring states, had to borrow the military tally to raise the siege of Zhao—without Lady Ruji's old grievance, that exploit would never have been heard of.

sentences.zh: 事有煩而不可省，費而不得已，蓋謂此也。

translations.literal: There are affairs troublesome yet that cannot be omitted, costly yet unavoidable—this is meant.
translations.idiomatic: Some burdens cannot be shunned and some costs must be paid—this is one of them.

sentences.zh: 』書奏，從之。

translations.literal: When the memorial was presented, the emperor followed his advice.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor approved the memorial.

sentences.zh: 詩身雖在外，盡心朝廷，讜言善策，隨事獻納。

translations.literal: Though Shi's person was outside the capital, his heart was wholly with the court; sound counsel and good plans he offered as occasions arose.
translations.idiomatic: Though Du Shi served away from the capital, his mind stayed with the court; he submitted candid advice and sound plans whenever occasion allowed.

sentences.zh: 視事七年，政化大行。

translations.literal: After seven years in office, his rule and transformation flourished greatly.
translations.idiomatic: After seven years in office his governance had transformed the region.

sentences.zh: 十四年，坐遣客為弟報仇，被徵，會病卒。

translations.literal: In the fourteenth year he was charged with sending a client to avenge his brother; he was summoned [to account], but fell ill and died before he arrived.
translations.idiomatic: In the fourteenth year he was implicated for sending a retainer to avenge his brother. He was summoned to the capital but took ill and died on the way.

sentences.zh: 司隸校尉鮑永上書言詩貧困無田宅，喪無所歸。

translations.literal: The colonel of the metropolitan bureau Bao Yong memorialized that Shi was poor and owned no land or house, and had nowhere to bury his body.
translations.idiomatic: The colonel of the metropolitan bureau, Bao Yong, reported that Du Shi had died penniless, without land or a house, and had no place for burial.

sentences.zh: 詔使治喪郡邸，賻絹千匹。

translations.literal: An edict ordered the funeral arranged at the commandery hostel in the capital and granted a thousand bolts of silk for burial expenses.
translations.idiomatic: An edict ordered his funeral held at the commandery hostel in the capital and granted a thousand bolts of silk toward the expenses.

sentences.zh: 孔奮字君魚，扶風茂陵人也。

translations.literal: Kong Fen, courtesy name Junyu, was a man of Maoling in Fufeng.
translations.idiomatic: Kong Fen, courtesy name Junyu, was a native of Maoling in Fufeng.

sentences.zh: 曾祖霸，元帝時為侍中。

translations.literal: His great-grandfather Ba was a palace attendant under Emperor Yuan.
translations.idiomatic: His great-grandfather Ba had been a palace attendant under Emperor Yuan.

sentences.zh: 奮少從劉歆受《春秋左氏傳》，歆稱之，謂門人曰：『吾已從君魚受道矣。』

translations.literal: In youth Fen studied the Zuo Tradition of the Spring and Autumn Annals with Liu Xin; Xin praised him and said to his disciples, 'I have already received the Way from Junyu.'
translations.idiomatic: As a young man Kong Fen studied the Zuo commentary on the Spring and Autumn Annals under Liu Xin. Xin praised him and told his students, 'I have learned the Way from Junyu.'

sentences.zh: 遭王莽亂，奮與老母、幼弟避兵河西。

translations.literal: When Wang Mang's turmoil came, Fen fled with his aged mother and young brother to Hexi to escape the fighting.
translations.idiomatic: When Wang Mang's rebellion convulsed the realm, Kong Fen took his mother and younger brother west to Hexi to escape the war.

sentences.zh: 建武五年，河西大將軍竇融請奮署議曹掾，守姑臧長。

translations.literal: In the fifth year of Jianwu, the general-in-chief of Hexi Dou Rong summoned Fen to serve as adjunct of the deliberation bureau and as acting magistrate of Guzang.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianwu 5 Dou Rong, general-in-chief of Hexi, appointed him secretary of the deliberation bureau and acting magistrate of Guzang.

sentences.zh: 八年，賜爵關內侯。

translations.literal: In the eighth year he was granted the noble rank of full marquis within the passes.
translations.idiomatic: In the eighth year he received the rank of full marquis within the passes.

sentences.zh: 時天下擾亂，惟河西獨安，而姑臧稱為富邑，通貨羌胡，市日四合，每居縣者，不盈數月輒致豐積。

translations.literal: When the realm was in turmoil, only Hexi remained at peace, and Guzang was called a rich town; trade with the Qiang and Hu ran four market sessions a day, and whoever held office in the county generally grew wealthy within months.
translations.idiomatic: While the empire was in chaos, only Hexi stayed calm. Guzang was a wealthy entrepôt; Qiang and Hu traders exchanged goods in four market sessions a day, and a magistrate who stayed a few months usually left rich.

sentences.zh: 奮在職四年，財產無所增。

translations.literal: Fen held office four years and his property did not increase.
translations.idiomatic: Kong Fen held the post four years without adding a coin to his estate.

sentences.zh: 事母孝謹，雖為儉約，奉養極求珍膳。

translations.literal: He served his mother with filial care; though he lived frugally, he sought the finest foods for her support.
translations.idiomatic: He was a devoted son: though he lived plainly himself, he sought the best food for his mother.

sentences.zh: 躬率妻、子，同甘菜茹。

translations.literal: He personally led wife and children to share plain vegetables.
translations.idiomatic: He and his wife and children ate the same coarse greens.

sentences.zh: 時天下未定，士多不修節操，而奮力行清潔，為眾人所笑，或以為身處脂膏，不能以自潤，徒益苦辛耳。

translations.literal: When the realm was not yet settled, most scholars neglected integrity; yet Fen strove for purity and was laughed at by the crowd, or people thought that though he stood in the fat of the land he could not enrich himself and was only increasing his hardship in vain.
translations.idiomatic: In unsettled times few gentlemen cared about integrity. Kong Fen insisted on spotless conduct; people mocked him or said he sat in the fat of the land yet never greased his own palms—only making life harder for nothing.

sentences.zh: 奮既立節，治貴仁平，太守梁統深相敬待，不以官屬禮之，常迎於大門，引入見母。

translations.literal: Once Fen had established his integrity, his administration valued kindness and fairness; the governor Liang Tong treated him with deep respect, not as a subordinate, and always met him at the great gate and led him in to see his mother.
translations.idiomatic: Once his integrity was known, he governed with humanity and evenhandedness. Governor Liang Tong held him in the highest regard, refused to treat him as a mere subordinate, met him at the main gate, and often brought him inside to meet his own mother.

sentences.zh: 隴蜀既平，河西守令咸被徵召，財貨連轂，彌竟川澤。

translations.literal: After Long and Shu were pacified, all the frontier commanders and magistrates were summoned; carts loaded with wealth stretched in strings and filled the streams.
translations.idiomatic: After Long and Shu were pacified, every Hexi official was recalled to court. Carts loaded with treasure lined up wheel to wheel and clogged the roads and fords.

sentences.zh: 惟奮無資，單車就路。

translations.literal: Only Fen had no resources; he set out alone in a single cart.
translations.idiomatic: Kong Fen alone had no baggage: he climbed into a single cart and set off.

sentences.zh: 姑臧吏民及羌胡更相謂曰：『孔君清廉仁賢，舉縣蒙恩，如何今去，不共報德！

translations.literal: The officials and people of Guzang and the Qiang and Hu said to one another, 'Lord Kong is pure, honest, benevolent, and worthy; the whole county has received his grace—how can he leave now, and we not repay his virtue together!'
translations.idiomatic: Officials and commoners in Guzang, Qiang, and Hu traders said to one another, 'Magistrate Kong is incorrupt, humane, and wise; the whole county owes him—how can we let him go without returning his kindness?'

sentences.zh: 』遂相賦斂牛、馬、器物千萬以上，追送數百里。

translations.literal: Thereupon they levied one another for cattle, horses, and goods worth over ten million cash and escorted him in pursuit for several hundred li.
translations.idiomatic: So they pooled cattle, horses, and goods worth millions of cash and followed him for hundreds of li to see him off.

sentences.zh: 奮謝之而已，一無所受。

translations.literal: Fen declined them and that was all; he accepted nothing.
translations.idiomatic: Kong Fen thanked them but refused every gift.

sentences.zh: 既至京師，除武都郡丞。

translations.literal: When he reached the capital, he was appointed assistant governor of Wudu commandery.
translations.idiomatic: On reaching the capital he was named assistant governor of Wudu.

sentences.zh: 時，隴西餘賊隗茂等夜攻府舍，殘殺郡守，賊畏奮追急，乃執其妻子，欲以為質。

translations.literal: At that time the remnant bandits of Longxi, Wei Mao and others, attacked the headquarters by night, cruelly killed the governor; the bandits feared Fen's hot pursuit and seized his wife and children, intending to use them as hostages.
translations.idiomatic: Bandit remnants in Longxi led by Wei Mao stormed the yamen at night and murdered the governor. Fearing Kong Fen's pursuit, they seized his wife and son as hostages.

sentences.zh: 奮年已五十，唯有一子，終不顧望，遂窮力討之。

translations.literal: Fen was already fifty years old and had only one son, yet to the end he did not look back [to spare them] and thereupon exhausted his strength in attacking them.
translations.idiomatic: Fen was fifty and had only one son, but he never wavered and threw every resource into destroying the bandits.

sentences.zh: 吏民感義，莫不倍用命焉。

translations.literal: The officials and people were moved by his righteousness; none failed to redouble their obedience to orders.
translations.idiomatic: Touched by his integrity, officials and commoners obeyed his orders with redoubled zeal.

sentences.zh: 郡多氐人，便習山谷，其大豪齊鐘留者，為群氐所信向。

translations.literal: The commandery had many Di people, skilled in the mountains and valleys; their great chieftain Qi Zhongliu was trusted and followed by the Di bands.
translations.idiomatic: The commandery was full of Di who knew the hills. Their leading headman, Qi Zhongliu, commanded the trust of the Di communities.

sentences.zh: 奮乃率厲鐘留等令要遮抄擊，共為表裏。

translations.literal: Fen thereupon led and urged Zhongliu and the rest, ordering them to intercept, ambush, and strike raiding parties, acting as inner and outer in coordination.
translations.idiomatic: Kong Fen rallied Zhongliu and his men to set ambushes and hit-and-run attacks, coordinating strikes from within and without.

sentences.zh: 賊窘懼逼急，乃推奮妻子以置軍前，冀當退卻，而擊之愈厲，遂禽滅茂等，奮妻、子亦為所殺。

translations.literal: The bandits, hard-pressed and fearful, pushed Fen's wife and children before the army, hoping he would pull back, but he struck them all the harder; thereupon he captured and destroyed Mao and the rest, and Fen's wife and child were also killed by them.
translations.idiomatic: Cornered, the bandits shoved his family in front of their lines, hoping he would relent. He struck harder instead, wiped out Wei Mao's band, and lost his wife and son in the fighting.

sentences.zh: 世祖下詔褒美，拜為武都太守。

translations.literal: Shizu issued an edict praising his beauty of conduct and appointed him governor of Wudu.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Guangwu issued an edict of praise and appointed him governor of Wudu.

sentences.zh: 奮自為府丞，已見敬重，及拜太守，舉郡莫不改操。

translations.literal: From the time Fen was assistant governor he was already treated with respect; when he was appointed governor, everyone in the commandery changed their conduct.
translations.idiomatic: He had earned respect as assistant governor; once he became governor, the whole commandery mended its ways.

sentences.zh: 為政明斷，甄善疾非，見有美德，愛之如親，其無行者，忿之若仇，郡中稱為清平。

translations.literal: In administration he was clear and decisive; he distinguished good and hated evil—if he saw someone with fine virtue he loved him like kin; if someone lacked conduct he resented him like an enemy; the commandery called him pure and even.
translations.idiomatic: He governed with clear judgment, rewarding the worthy and punishing wrong. He cherished men of virtue like family and treated the corrupt like foes. The commandery called his rule fair and clean.

sentences.zh: 弟奇，遊學洛陽。

translations.literal: His younger brother Qi studied as a wandering scholar in Luoyang.
translations.idiomatic: His brother Qi went to Luoyang to study.

sentences.zh: 奮以奇經明當仕，上病去官，守約鄉閭，卒於家。

translations.literal: Fen, because Qi was clear in the classics and ought to take office, submitted a memorial on illness and left office, kept restraint in his village, and died at home.
translations.idiomatic: Wanting the classically trained Qi to take office, Kong Fen resigned on grounds of illness, lived quietly in his village, and died at home.

sentences.zh: 奇博通經典，作《春秋左氏刪》。

translations.literal: Qi broadly mastered the classics and composed the Excision of the Zuo Tradition of the Spring and Autumn Annals.
translations.idiomatic: Qi mastered the classics and wrote the Excision of the Zuo commentary.

sentences.zh: 奮晚有子嘉，官至城門校尉，作《左氏說》雲。

translations.literal: Fen late in life had a son Jia, whose office rose to colonel of the city gates; he composed Explications of the Zuo Tradition, it is said.
translations.idiomatic: Kong Fen's son Jia, born late in his life, rose to colonel of the city gates and wrote Explications of the Zuo Tradition.

sentences.zh: 張堪字君游，南陽宛人也，為郡族姓。

translations.literal: Zhang Kan, courtesy name Junyou, was a man of Wan in Nanyang; he was a great clan surname of the commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Kan, courtesy name Junyou, came from Wan in Nanyang and belonged to one of the commandery's leading families.

sentences.zh: 堪早孤。

translations.literal: Kan was orphaned early.
translations.idiomatic: He lost his father while still young.

sentences.zh: 讓先父餘財數百萬與兄子。

translations.literal: He yielded his late father's remaining property of several million cash to his elder brother's son.
translations.idiomatic: He gave his late father's estate, worth millions, to his brother's son.

sentences.zh: 年十六，受業長安，誌美行厲，諸儒號曰『聖童』。

translations.literal: At age sixteen he took instruction in Chang'an; his aims were fine and his conduct stern; the scholars called him the 'sage boy.'
translations.idiomatic: At sixteen he studied in Chang'an, with lofty aims and strict conduct; the scholars dubbed him the 'sage child.'

sentences.zh: 世祖微時，見堪誌操，常嘉焉。

translations.literal: When Shizu was still obscure, he saw Kan's aims and integrity and often praised him.
translations.idiomatic: When Emperor Guangwu was still a commoner, he noticed Zhang Kan's character and often praised him.

sentences.zh: 及即位，中郎將來歙薦堪，召拜郎中，三遷為謁者。

translations.literal: When he ascended the throne, the general of the gentlemen Lai Xi recommended Kan; he was summoned and appointed gentleman of the palace, and after three promotions became usher.
translations.idiomatic: After his accession, General Lai Xi recommended Kan. He was appointed gentleman of the palace and after three promotions became an imperial usher.

sentences.zh: 使送委輸縑帛，並領騎七千匹，詣大司馬吳漢伐公孫述，在道追拜蜀郡太守。

translations.literal: He was sent to deliver tribute silk and cloth, and concurrently led seven thousand cavalry mounts, proceeding to grand marshal Wu Han's campaign against Gongsun Shu; on the way he was promoted to governor of Shu commandery.
translations.idiomatic: He was ordered to deliver silk tribute and seven thousand mounts to Grand Marshal Wu Han for the campaign against Gongsun Shu. En route he was appointed governor of Shu commandery.

sentences.zh: 時漢軍餘七日糧，陰具船欲遁去。

translations.literal: At that time Han's army had grain for only seven days left; secretly they prepared boats intending to withdraw.
translations.idiomatic: Wu Han's army had only seven days' rations; he quietly fitted out boats for a retreat.

sentences.zh: 堪聞之，馳往見漢，說述必敗，不宜退師之策。

translations.literal: When Kan heard it, he galloped to see Han and argued that Shu was sure to be defeated and that withdrawing the army was not the right policy.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Kan rode to Wu Han and argued that Gongsun Shu was doomed and retreating would be a mistake.

sentences.zh: 漢從之，乃示弱挑敵，述果自出，戰死城下。

translations.literal: Han followed him; thereupon they feigned weakness to provoke the enemy, and Shu indeed came out in person and died in battle below the walls.
translations.idiomatic: Wu Han agreed. They feigned weakness to lure Gongsun Shu out; Shu sallied forth and fell beneath the city walls.

sentences.zh: 成都既拔，堪先入據其城，撿閱庫藏，收其珍寶，悉條列上言，秋毫無私。

translations.literal: When Chengdu had been taken, Kan was first to enter and occupy the city; he inspected the treasuries, collected the precious goods, listed them all in a memorial upward, and not a hair's breadth was for himself.
translations.idiomatic: When Chengdu fell, Zhang Kan was first into the city. He inventoried the storehouses and valuables, reported every item to the throne, and kept nothing for himself.

sentences.zh: 慰撫吏民，蜀人大悅。

translations.literal: He comforted officials and people, and the people of Shu were greatly pleased.
translations.idiomatic: He reassured officials and commoners, and Shu rejoiced.

sentences.zh: 在郡二年，徵拜騎都尉，後領票騎將軍杜茂營，擊破匈奴於高柳，拜漁陽太守。

translations.literal: After two years in the commandery he was summoned and appointed commandant of cavalry; later he took command of general of agile cavalry Du Mao's camp, defeated the Xiongnu at Gaoliu, and was appointed governor of Yuyang.
translations.idiomatic: After two years he was recalled as commandant of cavalry, then led General Du Mao's column, routed the Xiongnu at Gaoliu, and was named governor of Yuyang.

sentences.zh: 捕擊奸猾，賞罰必信，吏民皆樂為用。

translations.literal: He arrested and struck the crafty and slippery; rewards and punishments were sure and trustworthy; officials and people all gladly served him.
translations.idiomatic: He cracked down on wrongdoers; rewards and punishments were reliable, and everyone was glad to serve him.

sentences.zh: 匈奴嘗以萬騎入漁陽，堪率數千騎奔擊，大破之，郡界以靜。

translations.literal: The Xiongnu once entered Yuyang with ten thousand cavalry; Kan led several thousand cavalry in a dash attack and greatly defeated them; the commandery border was thereby quiet.
translations.idiomatic: When the Xiongnu once poured into Yuyang with ten thousand horsemen, Zhang Kan counterattacked with a few thousand and routed them; the frontier grew calm.

sentences.zh: 乃於狐奴開稻田八千餘頃，勸民耕種，以致殷富。

translations.literal: Thereupon at Hunü he opened over eight thousand qing of paddy fields, urged the people to plow and plant, and thereby brought about abundance and wealth.
translations.idiomatic: At Hunü he opened more than eight thousand qing of rice land, encouraged farming, and brought the commandery prosperity.

sentences.zh: 百姓歌曰：『桑無附枝，麥穗兩岐。

translations.literal: The people sang: 'The mulberry has no extra branches; the wheat ears fork in two.'
translations.idiomatic: The people sang: 'Mulberries grow straight without side shoots; wheat bears double ears.'

sentences.zh: 張君為政，樂不可支。

translations.literal: Lord Zhang holds government; joy cannot be sustained [i.e. is overwhelming].'
translations.idiomatic: Under Magistrate Zhang we are so happy we can hardly bear it.'

sentences.zh: 』視事八年，匈奴不敢犯塞。

translations.literal: After eight years in office, the Xiongnu did not dare violate the frontier.
translations.idiomatic: For eight years in office the Xiongnu dared not raid the passes.

sentences.zh: 帝嘗召見諸郡計吏，問其風土及前後守令能否。

translations.literal: The emperor once summoned the clerks of the commandery accounts for audience and asked them about local customs and whether former and later governors and magistrates had been capable or not.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor once received the commandery accounting clerks and asked about local customs and how past governors had performed.

sentences.zh: 蜀郡計掾樊顯進曰：『漁陽太守張堪昔在蜀，其仁以惠下，威能討奸。

translations.literal: The accounting clerk of Shu commandery, Fan Xian, advanced and said, 'The governor of Yuyang, Zhang Kan, when formerly in Shu, by benevolence favored those below, and by awe was able to punish the wicked.'
translations.idiomatic: Fan Xian, clerk of Shu commandery, said, 'When Zhang Kan governed Shu, kindness reached the people and authority checked wrongdoing.'

sentences.zh: 前公孫述破時，珍寶山積，捲握之物，足富十世，而堪去職之日，乘折轅車，布被囊而已。

translations.literal: When earlier Gongsun Shu was broken, precious goods piled like mountains; what could be rolled up in the fist was enough to enrich ten generations, yet on the day Kan left office he rode a cart with a broken axle, a cloth quilt, and a bag—that was all.
translations.idiomatic: When Gongsun Shu fell, treasure enough for ten generations lay heaped in Chengdu, yet the day Zhang Kan departed he rode out in a broken-down cart with only a cloth bundle for luggage.'

sentences.zh: 』帝聞，良久嘆息，拜顯為魚復長。

translations.literal: The emperor heard and sighed long; he appointed Xian magistrate of Yufu.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor listened in silence, then sighed deeply and appointed Fan Xian magistrate of Yufu.

sentences.zh: 方徵堪，會病卒，帝深悼惜之，下詔褒揚，賜帛百匹。

translations.literal: Just as he was about to summon Kan, Kan happened to die of illness; the emperor deeply mourned and pitied him, issued an edict of praise, and granted a hundred bolts of silk.
translations.idiomatic: He was about to summon Zhang Kan when Kan died. The emperor grieved, issued a laudatory edict, and granted a hundred bolts of silk.

sentences.zh: 廉範字叔度，京兆杜陵人也，趙將廉頗之後也。

translations.literal: Lian Fan, courtesy name Shudu, was a man of Duling in the capital district; he was a descendant of the Zhao general Lian Po.
translations.idiomatic: Lian Fan, courtesy name Shudu, came from Duling in the capital region and traced his line to the Zhao general Lian Po.

sentences.zh: 漢興，以廉氏豪宗，自苦陘徙焉。

translations.literal: When Han rose, because the Lian clan was a powerful lineage, they moved from Kuxing to there.
translations.idiomatic: At the rise of the Han, the powerful Lian clan moved from Kuxing to that region.

sentences.zh: 世為邊郡守，或葬隴西襄武，故因仕焉。

translations.literal: For generations they had been frontier commandery governors; some were buried in Xiangwu in Longxi, and therefore [the family] took office there.
translations.idiomatic: For generations they had served as frontier governors; some were buried at Xiangwu in Longxi, so the family settled there.

sentences.zh: 曾祖父褒，成、哀間為右將軍，祖父丹，王莽時為大司馬庸部牧，皆有名前世。

translations.literal: His great-grandfather Bao was general of the right between Cheng and Ai; his grandfather Dan was grand marshal and shepherd of the Yong department under Wang Mang—all had renown in former generations.
translations.idiomatic: His great-grandfather Bao was general of the right under Emperors Cheng and Ai; his grandfather Dan was grand marshal and Yong department shepherd under Wang Mang. Both were famous in their day.

sentences.zh: 範父遭喪亂，客死於蜀漢，範遂流寓西州。

translations.literal: Fan's father encountered the turmoil of collapse and died as a guest in Shu-Han; Fan thereupon drifted and sojourned in the western province.
translations.idiomatic: Fan's father died a wanderer in Shu during the wars, and Lian Fan took refuge in the west.

sentences.zh: 西州平，歸鄉裏。

translations.literal: When the western province was pacified, he returned to his village.
translations.idiomatic: When the west was pacified, he went home.

sentences.zh: 年十五，辭母西迎父喪。

translations.literal: At age fifteen he bade farewell to his mother and went west to meet his father's coffin.
translations.idiomatic: At fifteen he left his mother to travel west and bring back his father's remains.

sentences.zh: 蜀郡太守張穆，丹之故吏，乃重資送範，範無所受，與客步負喪歸葭萌。

translations.literal: The governor of Shu commandery, Zhang Mu, had been Dan's former subordinate; he thereupon sent Fan heavy resources for the journey, but Fan accepted nothing and, with a guest, walked carrying the mourning on his back, returning to Jiameng.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Mu, governor of Shu and an old subordinate of Fan's grandfather, offered generous travel funds; Lian Fan refused and walked toward home with a companion, carrying the coffin toward Jiameng.

sentences.zh: 載船觸石破沒，範抱持棺柩，遂俱沈溺，眾傷其義，鉤求得之，療救僅免於死。

translations.literal: The ferry boat struck a rock, broke, and sank; Fan embraced and held the coffin, and thereupon both sank and drowned; the crowd was moved by his righteousness, hooked and sought them and got them out, and with healing and rescue he barely escaped death.
translations.idiomatic: The boat struck a rock and sank. Lian Fan clung to the coffin and went under with it. Onlookers, moved by his devotion, fished them out and revived him at the last moment.

sentences.zh: 穆聞，復馳遣使持前資物追範，範又固辭。

translations.literal: When Mu heard, he again urgently sent a messenger bearing the earlier goods to catch up with Fan; Fan again firmly declined.
translations.idiomatic: When Zhang Mu heard, he sent the same gifts after him; Fan again refused.

sentences.zh: 歸葬服竟，詣京師受業，事博士薛漢。

translations.literal: When the burial was done and mourning complete, he went to the capital to receive instruction and served erudite Xue Han.
translations.idiomatic: After burying his father and finishing mourning, he studied in the capital under Erudite Xue Han.

sentences.zh: 京兆、隴西二郡更請召，皆不應，永平初，隴西太守鄧融備禮謁範為功曹，會融為州所舉案，範知事譴難解，欲以權相濟，乃托病求去，融不達其意，大恨之。

translations.literal: The two commanderies of the capital district and Longxi repeatedly invited and summoned him; he did not respond to any. At the beginning of Yongping, the governor of Longxi, Deng Rong, with full ceremony visited Fan and appointed him merit clerk; it happened that Rong was impeached by the province; Fan knew the affair's blame would be hard to resolve and wished to help him with a stratagem; thereupon he pleaded illness and asked to leave; Rong did not grasp his intent and greatly resented him.
translations.idiomatic: Both the capital region and Longxi wanted him; he declined. Early in Yongping, Governor Deng Rong of Longxi ceremoniously named him merit clerk. When Rong was impeached by the provincial authorities, Fan saw no easy way out and hoped to help by guile. He resigned on grounds of illness; Rong did not understand and bitterly resented him.

sentences.zh: 範於是東至洛陽，變名姓，求代廷尉獄卒。

translations.literal: Fan thereupon went east to Luoyang, changed his name, and sought to substitute as a jailer of the commandant of justice.
translations.idiomatic: Fan went east to Luoyang under an assumed name and took work as a jailer in the commandant of justice's prison.

sentences.zh: 居無幾，融果徵下獄，範遂得衛侍左右，盡心勤勞。

translations.literal: Before long, Rong was indeed summoned and cast into prison; Fan thereupon was able to guard and attend at his side, exhausting his heart in diligent toil.
translations.idiomatic: Soon Rong was arrested and jailed. Fan stayed at his side as guard and attendant, serving him tirelessly.

sentences.zh: 融怪其貌類範而殊不意，乃謂曰：『卿何似我故功曹邪？

translations.literal: Rong marveled that his appearance resembled Fan yet did not expect it at all, and said to him, 'Why do you resemble my former merit clerk?'
translations.idiomatic: Rong thought he looked like Fan but could not believe it. 'Why do you look so much like my old merit clerk?'

sentences.zh: 』範訶之曰：『君困厄瞀亂邪！

translations.literal: Fan scolded him, saying, 'Are you muddled in hardship and confusion!'
translations.idiomatic: Fan snapped, 'Have troubles turned your wits?'

sentences.zh: 』語遂絕。

translations.literal: The words thereupon ceased.
translations.idiomatic: He said no more.

sentences.zh: 融系出因病，範隨而養視，及死，竟不言，身自將車送喪致南陽，葬畢乃去。

translations.literal: Rong was released on account of illness; Fan followed and nursed and watched him; when he died, to the end he did not speak; he personally drove the cart sending the mourning to Nanyang, and only when burial was done did he leave.
translations.idiomatic: Rong was released when he fell ill. Fan nursed him and, when he died, still never revealed himself. He drove the hearse to Nanyang himself and left only after the burial.

sentences.zh: 後辟公府，會薛漢坐楚王事誅，故人門生莫敢視，範獨往收斂之。

translations.literal: Later he was summoned to the public office; it happened that Xue Han was executed for the Prince of Chu affair; old friends and disciples none dared look; Fan alone went to gather and enshroud him.
translations.idiomatic: Later he took a post in the ministerial bureau. When Xue Han was executed for the Prince of Chu case, former friends and students stayed away; only Fan claimed the body.

sentences.zh: 吏以聞，顯宗大怒，召範入，詰責曰：『薛漢與楚王同謀，交亂天下，範公府掾，不與朝廷同心，而反收斂罪人，何也？

translations.literal: The clerks reported it; Emperor Ming was greatly angry, summoned Fan to enter, and interrogated him, saying, 'Xue Han plotted with the Prince of Chu and threw the realm into confusion; Fan, as a bureau clerk, was not of one mind with the court, yet on the contrary gathered in a criminal—why?'
translations.idiomatic: Officials reported it. Emperor Ming summoned Fan and demanded, 'Xue Han conspired with the Prince of Chu to throw the realm into chaos. You were a bureau clerk—you should stand with the court—yet you buried a condemned traitor. Why?'

sentences.zh: 』範叩頭曰：『臣無狀愚戇，以為漢等皆已伏誅，不勝師資之情，罪當萬坐。

translations.literal: Fan knocked his head and said, 'Your servant lacks form, foolish and blunt; I thought Han and the rest had already suffered execution; I could not overcome the feeling of teacher and student—the crime deserves ten thousand deaths.'
translations.idiomatic: Fan kowtowed. 'I was foolish. I thought that once Xue Han was executed, my debt to my teacher overcame me. I deserve death ten thousand times over.'

sentences.zh: 』帝怒稍解，問範曰：『卿廉頗後邪？

translations.literal: The emperor's anger slightly eased; he asked Fan, 'Are you a descendant of Lian Po?'
translations.idiomatic: The emperor's anger cooled. 'Are you descended from Lian Po?'

sentences.zh: 與右將軍褒、大司馬丹有親屬乎？

translations.literal: Do you have kinship with the general of the right Bao and the grand marshal Dan?'
translations.idiomatic: Are you kin to General of the Right Bao and Grand Marshal Dan?'

sentences.zh: 』範對曰：『褒，臣之曾祖；

translations.literal: Fan replied, 'Bao is your servant's great-grandfather;'
translations.idiomatic: Fan said, 'Bao was my great-grandfather;'

sentences.zh: 丹，臣之祖也。

translations.literal: Dan is your servant's grandfather.'
translations.idiomatic: Dan was my grandfather.'

sentences.zh: 帝曰：『怪卿誌膽敢爾！

translations.literal: The emperor said, 'Strange that your aims and gall dare thus!'
translations.idiomatic: The emperor said, 'No wonder you have the nerve to act like this!'

sentences.zh: 』因貰之。

translations.literal: Thereupon he pardoned him.
translations.idiomatic: He pardoned him.

sentences.zh: 由是顯名。

translations.literal: From this his name became manifest.
translations.idiomatic: He became famous overnight.

sentences.zh: 舉茂才，數月，再遷為雲中太守。

translations.literal: He was recommended as flourishing talent; within several months he was transferred again to be governor of Yunzhong.
translations.idiomatic: Recommended as flourishing talent, within months he was promoted to governor of Yunzhong.

sentences.zh: 會匈奴大入塞，烽火日通。

translations.literal: It happened that the Xiongnu entered the passes in great force; beacon fires communicated daily.
translations.idiomatic: The Xiongnu poured through the frontier; beacons blazed day after day.

sentences.zh: 故事，虜入過五千人，移書傍郡。

translations.literal: By precedent, when the barbarians entered in excess of five thousand persons, letters were shifted to neighboring commanderies.
translations.idiomatic: By regulation, if the enemy crossed in strength above five thousand, the governor was to notify neighboring commanderies.

sentences.zh: 吏欲傳檄求救，範不聽，自率士卒拒之。

translations.literal: The clerks wished to circulate a call for help; Fan would not listen and personally led soldiers and clerks to resist them.
translations.idiomatic: His staff wanted to send out calls for reinforcements. Fan refused and led his own men to meet the attack.

sentences.zh: 虜眾盛而範兵不敵。

translations.literal: The barbarian host was strong and Fan's troops did not match them.
translations.idiomatic: The enemy far outnumbered him.

sentences.zh: 會日暮，令軍士各交縛兩炬，三頭□火，營中星列。

translations.literal: When evening came, he ordered the soldiers each to bind two torches crosswise, three heads [text lacuna] fire, and in the camp they were arrayed like stars.
translations.idiomatic: At dusk he had each soldier cross-bind two torches so that three flames burned from each bundle (the third character is missing in the received text) and arrayed them through camp like stars.

sentences.zh: 虜遙望火多，謂漢兵救至，大驚。

translations.literal: The barbarians looked from afar at the many fires and thought Han reinforcements had arrived; they were greatly alarmed.
translations.idiomatic: From a distance the Xiongnu saw countless fires and assumed Han reinforcements had arrived; they panicked.

sentences.zh: 待旦將退，範乃令軍中蓐食，晨往赴之，斬首數百級，虜自相轔藉，死者千餘人，由此不敢復向雲中。

translations.literal: Waiting for dawn they were about to withdraw; Fan thereupon ordered the army to eat at the mat [before dawn], and at morning went to attack them; several hundred heads were cut off; the barbarians trampled one another; over a thousand died; from this they did not again dare turn toward Yunzhong.
translations.idiomatic: They waited for dawn to pull back. Fan had his men eat before daylight, then struck at first light. Hundreds fell; the Xiongnu trampled one another and over a thousand died. They never again dared threaten Yunzhong.

sentences.zh: 後頻歷武威、武都二郡太守，隨俗化導，各得治宜。

translations.literal: Later he successively served as governor of Wuwei and Wudu commanderies, following custom in transforming and guiding, each obtaining what was fitting for rule.
translations.idiomatic: He later governed Wuwei and Wudu in turn, adapting policy to local custom in each place.

sentences.zh: 建中初，遷蜀郡太守，其俗尚文辯，好相持短長，範每厲以淳厚，不受偷薄之說。

translations.literal: At the beginning of Jianzhong, he was promoted to governor of Shu commandery; its custom esteemed literary disputation and liked to hold to comparative fault-finding; Fan always urged them with simplicity and honesty and did not accept mean and shallow discourse.
translations.idiomatic: At the opening of the Jianchu period (the received text reads Jianzhong at the start of the line) he became governor of Shu, where people loved rhetoric and petty faultfinding. Fan urged simplicity and refused to indulge shallow talk.

sentences.zh: 成都民物豐盛，邑宇逼側，舊制禁民夜作，以防火災，而更相隱蔽，燒者日屬。

translations.literal: Chengdu's people and goods were abundant; the town dwellings were cramped; the old system forbade the people to work at night to prevent fire disaster, but they concealed one another more and more, and those who burned increased daily.
translations.idiomatic: Chengdu was crowded and prosperous. An old curfew banned night work to prevent fires, but people worked in secret and fires grew more frequent.

sentences.zh: 範乃毀削先令，但嚴使儲水而已。

translations.literal: Fan thereupon destroyed and cut away the former order, only strictly making them store water—that was all.
translations.idiomatic: Fan repealed the old ban and simply required households to keep water ready for firefighting.

sentences.zh: 百姓為便，乃歌之曰：『廉叔度，來何墓？

translations.literal: The people found it convenient and therefore sang of him: 'Uncle Lian Shudu, why the tomb?' (The character for tomb is commonly emended to the homophone for evening, yielding 'why so late').
translations.idiomatic: The people were delighted and sang: 'Uncle Lian Shudu, why did you come so late?' (The word written as tomb is a pun on evening in the song.)

sentences.zh: 不禁火，民安作。

translations.literal: We are not forbidden fire; the people peacefully work.'
translations.idiomatic: No curfew on fires—we can work in peace.'

sentences.zh: 平生無襦今五絝。

translations.literal: All their lives no unlined coat—now five pairs of trousers.'
translations.idiomatic: Once we had no coat to our names—now we own five pairs of trousers.'

sentences.zh: 』在蜀數年，坐法免歸鄉裏。

translations.literal: After several years in Shu, he was convicted under the law, dismissed, and returned to his village.
translations.idiomatic: After several years in Shu he was convicted, stripped of office, and sent home.

sentences.zh: 範世在邊，廣田地，積財粟，悉以賑宗族朋友。

translations.literal: Fan's generations had been on the frontier; he extended fields and lands, accumulated wealth and grain, and used it all to relieve clan and friends.
translations.idiomatic: His family had long served on the frontier. He expanded his lands and stores and used the surplus to aid kin and friends.

sentences.zh: 肅宗崩，範奔赴敬陵。

translations.literal: When Emperor Zhang died, Fan rushed to Jingling.
translations.idiomatic: When Emperor Zhang died, Lian Fan hurried to Jingling for the funeral.

sentences.zh: 時廬江郡掾嚴麟奉章吊國，俱會於路。

translations.literal: At that time the clerk of Lujiang commandery, Yan Lin, carried a document to mourn the state; they both met on the road.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Lin, a clerk from Lujiang, was traveling to the capital on the same mourning mission; they met on the road.

sentences.zh: 麟乘小車，塗深馬死，不能自進，範見而湣然，命從騎下馬與之。

translations.literal: Lin rode a small cart; the road was deep and the horse died, and he could not advance on his own; Fan saw him and was moved to pity, ordered his mounted followers to dismount and give them to him.
translations.idiomatic: Lin's cart mired, his horse died, and he could not go on. Fan pitied him and gave him his escort's mounts.

sentences.zh: 不告而去。

translations.literal: Without telling him, he departed.
translations.idiomatic: He rode off without saying who he was.

sentences.zh: 麟事畢，不知馬所歸，乃緣蹤訪之。

translations.literal: When Lin's business was done, he did not know where the horses had gone; thereupon he followed tracks and visited to find him.
translations.idiomatic: When Lin finished his errand, he traced the horses to find their owner.

sentences.zh: 或謂麟曰：『故蜀郡太守廉叔度，好周人窮急，今奔國喪，獨單是耳。

translations.literal: Someone said to Lin, 'The former governor of Shu commandery, Lian Shudu, likes to relieve people in extremity; now he rushes to the national mourning—only alone thus.'
translations.idiomatic: Someone told him, 'That was the former governor of Shu, Lian Fan—he always helps people in need. He is on his way to the imperial funeral alone, just like that.'

sentences.zh: 』麟亦素聞範名，以為然，即牽馬造門，謝而歸之。

translations.literal: Lin also had long heard Fan's name, thought it was so, thereupon led the horses to his gate, thanked him, and returned them.
translations.idiomatic: Lin had heard of Fan. He brought the horses to Fan's door, thanked him, and returned them.

sentences.zh: 世伏其好義，然依倚大將軍竇憲，以此為譏。

translations.literal: The age submitted to his love of righteousness, yet he leaned on grand general Dou Xian, and for this was ridiculed.
translations.idiomatic: People admired his chivalry but criticized his closeness to General-in-chief Dou Xian.

sentences.zh: 卒於家。

translations.literal: He died at home.
translations.idiomatic: He died at home.

sentences.zh: 初，範與洛陽慶鴻為刎頸交，時人稱曰：『前有管、鮑，後有慶、廉。

translations.literal: Earlier, Fan and Qing Hong of Luoyang were friends who would cut their necks; people of the time said: 'Earlier there were Guan and Bao; later there are Qing and Lian.'
translations.idiomatic: Fan and Qing Hong of Luoyang were sworn friends. People said, 'After Guan Zhong and Bao Shuya come Qing Hong and Lian Fan.'

sentences.zh: 』鴻慷慨有義節，位至瑯邪、會稽二郡太守，所在有異跡。

translations.literal: Hong was magnanimous and had righteous integrity; his position reached governor of the two commanderies Langye and Kuaiji; wherever he was there were unusual traces.
translations.idiomatic: Hong was high-minded and principled. He rose to governor of Langye and Kuaiji and left a remarkable record in each post.

sentences.zh: 論曰：『張堪、廉範皆以氣俠立名，觀其振危急，赴險厄，有足壯者。

translations.literal: The treatise says: 'Zhang Kan and Lian Fan both established their names by chivalrous spirit; observe how they roused the desperate and went to perilous straits—there is enough to make one bold.'
translations.idiomatic: The historian's judgment: Zhang Kan and Lian Fan won renown through chivalrous spirit. They lifted others from danger and braved hardship themselves—enough to stir the reader.

sentences.zh: 堪之臨財，範之忘施，亦足以信意而感物矣。

translations.literal: Kan's facing wealth, Fan's forgetting to give—also enough to make intentions trustworthy and move things.
translations.idiomatic: Kan's integrity before riches and Fan's anonymous kindness speak to the heart.

sentences.zh: 若夫高祖之召欒布，明帝之引廉範，加怒以發其誌，就戮更延其寵，聞義能徙，誠君道所尚，然情理之樞，亦有開塞之感焉。

translations.literal: As for Gaozu's summoning Luan Bu, Mingdi's drawing in Lian Fan—adding anger to draw out their aims, facing execution yet further extending their favor—hearing righteousness and being able to change is truly what the way of the ruler esteems, yet the pivot of feeling and principle also has a sense of opening and closing.
translations.idiomatic: Gaozu summoned Luan Bu; Mingdi drew in Lian Fan—anger drew out their true mettle, and mercy followed the blade. A ruler who hears right and changes course follows the highest ideal, yet the hinge of human feeling opens and closes in ways that still move us.'

sentences.zh: 王堂字敬伯，廣漢郪人也。

translations.literal: Wang Tang, courtesy name Jingbo, was a man of Qi in Guanghan.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Tang, courtesy name Jingbo, came from Qi in Guanghan commandery.

sentences.zh: 初舉光祿茂才，遷穀城令，治有名跡。

translations.literal: At first he was recommended as flourishing talent by the minister of the household; he was promoted to magistrate of Gucheng, and his rule left a notable record.
translations.idiomatic: He was first recommended as flourishing talent by the minister of the household, then became magistrate of Gucheng, where his administration won notice.

sentences.zh: 永初中，西羌寇巴郡，為民患，詔書遣中郎將尹就攻討，連年不克。

translations.literal: In the Yongchu period, the western Qiang raided Ba commandery and became a scourge to the people; an edict sent the general of the gentlemen Yin Jiu to attack and punish them, but for successive years he could not overcome them.
translations.idiomatic: Early in Yongchu the western Qiang plagued Ba commandery. The court sent General Yin Jiu against them year after year without success.

sentences.zh: 三府舉堂治劇，拜巴郡太守。

translations.literal: The three offices recommended Tang to govern a difficult region; he was appointed governor of Ba commandery.
translations.idiomatic: The three highest bureaus nominated him as a man who could handle a crisis, and he was named governor of Ba.

sentences.zh: 堂馳兵赴賊，斬虜千餘級，巴、庸清靜，吏民生為立祠。

translations.literal: Tang rushed troops against the bandits and cut off over a thousand heads; Ba and Yong were cleared and still; officials and people built a shrine to him while he lived.
translations.idiomatic: Tang struck the enemy at speed and took more than a thousand heads. Ba and Yong grew calm, and the people erected a living shrine to him.

sentences.zh: 刺史張喬表其治能，遷右扶風。

translations.literal: Regional inspector Zhang Qiao memorialized his administrative ability; he was promoted to governor of You Fufeng.
translations.idiomatic: Inspector Zhang Qiao cited his competence, and he was promoted to governor of You Fufeng.

sentences.zh: 安帝西巡，阿母王聖、中常侍江京等並請屬於堂，堂不為用。

translations.literal: When Emperor An toured the west, the wet nurse Wang Sheng, the palace attendant Jiang Jing, and others all asked favors of Tang; Tang did not comply.
translations.idiomatic: On Emperor An's western tour, the emperor's nurse Wang Sheng and eunuch Jiang Jing all pressed Wang Tang for favors; he refused.

sentences.zh: 掾史固諫之，堂曰：『吾蒙國恩，豈可為權寵阿意，以死守之！

translations.literal: The clerks firmly remonstrated; Tang said, 'I have received the state's grace—how could I curry favor with the powerful and favored and hold to death for that!'
translations.idiomatic: His staff urged him to yield. Tang replied, 'The state has honored me—I will not toady to the powerful and their favorites, even on pain of death!'

sentences.zh: 』即日遣家屬歸，閉閤上病。

translations.literal: That same day he sent his family members home, closed his doors, and submitted a memorial claiming illness.
translations.idiomatic: That day he sent his household away, shut his gates, and memorialized that he was ill.

sentences.zh: 果有誣奏堂者，會帝崩，京等悉誅，堂以守正見稱。

translations.literal: There indeed were those who slanderously memorialized against Tang; it happened that the emperor died, and Jing and the rest were all executed; Tang was praised for holding to rectitude.
translations.idiomatic: False charges followed, but the emperor died before they could bite; Jiang Jing and his clique were executed, and Tang won praise for standing firm.

sentences.zh: 永建二年，徵入為將作大臣。

translations.literal: In the second year of Yongjian, he was summoned to court as minister of works.
translations.idiomatic: In Yongjian 2 he was recalled as minister of works.

sentences.zh: 四年，坐公事左轉議郎。

translations.literal: In the fourth year, for a public-affairs offense he was shifted left to gentleman consultant.
translations.idiomatic: In the fourth year a bureaucratic fault demoted him to gentleman consultant.

sentences.zh: 復拜魯相，政存簡一，至數年無辭訟。

translations.literal: He was again appointed chancellor of Lu; his government aimed at simplicity and unity, so that for several years there were no lawsuits.
translations.idiomatic: He was again named chancellor of Lu. His rule was plain and unified, and for years no one brought suit.

sentences.zh: 遷汝南太守，搜才禮士，不茍自專，乃教掾史曰：『古人勞於求賢，逸於任使，故能化清於上，事緝於下。

translations.literal: He was promoted to governor of Runan, sought out talent and treated scholars with courtesy, and was not arbitrary on his own authority; he instructed his clerks, saying, 'The ancients toiled in seeking the worthy and were at ease in employing them; therefore they could transform purity above and knit affairs below.'
translations.idiomatic: As governor of Runan he recruited talent and honored scholars, refusing to decide everything himself. He told his staff, 'The ancients wore themselves out finding good men, then ruled easily through them—purity at the top, order below.'

sentences.zh: 其憲章朝右，簡核才職，委功曹陳蕃。

translations.literal: As for establishing regulations for the court's right [officials], selecting and verifying talent and duties—entrust that to merit clerk Chen Fan.
translations.idiomatic: For drafting rules for high officials and vetting appointments, rely on merit clerk Chen Fan.

sentences.zh: 匡政理務，拾遺補闕，任主簿應嗣。

translations.literal: To rectify government and manage affairs, to pick up oversights and fill gaps—assign chief clerk Ying Si.
translations.idiomatic: For policy, administration, and plugging gaps in governance, assign chief clerk Ying Si.

sentences.zh: 庶循名責實，察言觀效焉。

translations.literal: Thus may we follow names and demand realities, inspect words and observe results.'
translations.idiomatic: Then we can match titles to deeds and judge men by what they achieve.'

sentences.zh: 』自是委誠求當，不復妄有辭教，郡內稱治。

translations.literal: From this he entrusted sincerity and sought what was fitting, no longer rashly issuing verbal instructions; the commandery was called well governed.
translations.idiomatic: After that he delegated in good faith and stopped issuing idle orders. The commandery was judged well run.

sentences.zh: 時大將軍梁商及尚書令袁湯，以求屬不行，並恨之。

translations.literal: At that time grand general Liang Shang and director of the secretariat Yuan Tang, because their requests for favor were not carried out, both resented him.
translations.idiomatic: Grand General Liang Shang and Director Yuan Tang both bore him a grudge when he turned down their patronage.

sentences.zh: 後廬江賊迸入弋陽界，堂勒兵追討，即便奔散，而商、湯猶因此風州奏堂在任無警，免歸家。

translations.literal: Later Lujiang bandits burst into the borders of Yiyang; Tang led troops in pursuit and attack, and they at once fled and scattered; yet Shang and Tang still for this reason prompted the province to memorialize that Tang had been without alert while in office, and he was dismissed and returned home.
translations.idiomatic: When Lujiang bandits spilled into Yiyang, Tang dispersed them at once. Even so, Liang Shang and Yuan Tang persuaded the provincial authorities to charge him with negligence in office, and he was sent home.

sentences.zh: 年八十六卒。

translations.literal: He died at age eighty-six.
translations.idiomatic: He died at eighty-six.

sentences.zh: 遺令薄斂，瓦棺以葬。

translations.literal: He left instructions for a thin burial, interment in a tile coffin.
translations.idiomatic: He ordered a plain funeral and burial in a tile coffin.

sentences.zh: 子稚，清行不仕。

translations.literal: His son Zhi, of pure conduct, did not take office.
translations.idiomatic: His son Zhi lived a clean life and never took office.

sentences.zh: 曾孫商，益州牧劉焉以為蜀郡太守，有治聲。

translations.literal: His great-grandson Shang—shepherd of Yizhou Liu Yan appointed him governor of Shu commandery; he had a reputation for good rule.
translations.idiomatic: His great-grandson Shang became governor of Shu under Liu Yan of Yizhou and was known as an able administrator.

sentences.zh: 蘇章字孺文，扶風平陵人也。

translations.literal: Su Zhang, courtesy name Ruwen, was a man of Pingling in Fufeng.
translations.idiomatic: Su Zhang, courtesy name Ruwen, came from Pingling in Fufeng.

sentences.zh: 八世祖建，武帝時為右將軍。

translations.literal: His eighth-generation ancestor Jian was general of the right under Emperor Wu.
translations.idiomatic: His eighth-generation ancestor Jian had been general of the right under Emperor Wu.

sentences.zh: 祖父純，字桓公，有高名，性強切而持毀譽，士友鹹憚之，至乃相謂曰：『見蘇桓公，患其教責人，不見，又思之。

translations.literal: His grandfather Chun, courtesy name Huanggong, had a high reputation; by nature he was forceful and cutting and held to praise and blame; scholar friends all feared him—to the point that they said to one another, 'When we see Su Huanggong, we worry at his lecturing and blaming people; when we do not see him, we miss him.'
translations.idiomatic: His grandfather Chun, known as Lord Huan of the Su clan, was famous, blunt, and severe; friends feared him yet said, 'Seeing Su Huan means a lecture, but not seeing him leaves us wanting.'

sentences.zh: 』三輔號為『大人』。

translations.literal: The three adjuncts titled him 'great man.'
translations.idiomatic: The capital region called him a 'great man.'

sentences.zh: 永平中，為奉車都尉竇固軍，出擊北匈奴、車師有功，封中陵鄉侯，官至南陽太守。

translations.literal: In the Yongping period he served in general of the chariots Dou Gu's army, went out to strike the northern Xiongnu and Jushi with merit, was enfeoffed as marquis of Zhongling village, and his office rose to governor of Nanyang.
translations.idiomatic: Under Yongping he campaigned with Dou Gu against the northern Xiongnu and Jushi, won a marquisate at Zhongling village, and rose to governor of Nanyang.

sentences.zh: 章少博學，能屬文。

translations.literal: Zhang in youth was broadly learned and able to compose texts.
translations.idiomatic: Su Zhang was widely read as a youth and wrote well.
